entities.name: artha
entities.cite: words become subordinate to the meaning

entities.name: lokaḥ
entities.cite: the world takes no cognizance of them

axioms.name: upasarjani
axioms.cite: The words become artha-upasarjani-bhuta — upasarjana is the grammarians' term for the subordinate member of a compound

axioms.name: pararthatva
axioms.cite: pararthatvat, existing for another's sake, like an instrument

denials.name: upasarjani
denials.cite: The words become artha-upasarjani-bhuta — upasarjana is the grammarians' term for the subordinate member of a compound

denials.name: pratipadyate
denials.cite: Hence lokah na pratipadyate, the world takes no cognizance of them

claims.name: upasarjani
claims.cite: words become subordinate to the meaning

rejected_readings.label: reversed_subordination
rejected_readings.rendering: In certain things-to-be-expressed, meanings become subordinate to the words, which remain focal to the listener's attention.
rejected_readings.why: Inverts the upasarjani relation; treats meanings rather than words as subordinate. This directly contradicts the verse's explicit statement that words become artha-upasarjani-bhuta (subordinate to meaning).

rejected_readings.label: world_attends_words
rejected_readings.rendering: The world clearly notices and attends to the words themselves, recognizing linguistic forms as focal to communication.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that the world attends to (pratipadyate) words, directly contradicting the verse's core claim that the world does not cognize the words when they are subordinate to meaning.

notes: The verse's restriction to 'certain cases' (kesucit) is acknowledged but not formalized as a modal operator; the axioms apply within the domain of subordinated words. The negation of pratipadyate (world does not attend) is captured via denial rather than positive axiom, since the verse defines the phenomenon precisely by what consciousness overlooks.
